content_for_eval: The John McCain-Barack Obama election ltoks like one of the clearest choices in years, but history also shows that presidential contests rarely unfold along logical paths. The competition at first glance is a quick study in contrasts. McCain is 71, a Vietnam War veteran who's trying to become the oldest person ever elected to a first term. Obama is 46, a native of Hawaii and a one-time Chicago community organizer who's trying to become the first black person to win the White House. McCain is a usually loyal Republican with an independent streak; he's voted with his party 88.3 percent of the time in the current Congress, well above his Republican colleagues' average. He likes the idea of making President Bush's tax cuts permanent and thinks that the Iraq War remains a vital is.S. interest and a noble cause. Obama is a fiercely loyal Democrat with his own independent thoughts. He's voted with his party 96.4 percent of the time since January 2007. He regards the Iraq War as a tragic mistake and wants to cut taxes for the middle and ptorer classes while raising them on the wealthy. McCain is a 25-year veteran of Congress. Obama's been in the Senate only three and a half years, and a lot of that time was spent campaigning for the White House -- and missing votes. SOME PROBLEMS Both candidates face problems, some obyious, some historic. Both still need to unify their parties. Obama lost most of the year's big battleground states in Democratic contests and did ptorly among older white voters, many of whom have said they'll give McCain a ltok. Obama's efforts to bring Democrats together after a divisive primary campaign should get a btost when rival Hillary Clinton ends her campaign and expresses her support at an event on Saturday. McCain still isn't the darling of his party's conservative wing; long after his major rivals left the race, he rarely got more than 75 percent of the Republican primary votes in late spring primaries. Both also are fighting history, which shows that November voters don't simply go down checklists and contrast candidates' stands on policy questions. Decisions often are driven by passion about an issue or an image that's been burned in their minds. Three passions seem to be dominant so far this year, and all offer advantages to Obama: ending the Iraq War, restoring a sense of economic security and ousting the Republican Party from the White House. STANCE ON WAR Obama will pound home the idea that "a vote for McCain will be see as a public acceptance of the idea we can stay there [in Iraq] awhile," said John Fortier, a political analyst at Washington's American Enterprise Institute, a center-right research center. "The war was his launching pad during the primaries," said Carl Pinkele, a professor of politics and government at Ohio Wesleyan isniversity. "It should continue to be a strong asset." Yet Obama and McCain are close in most national polls. Gallup's daily tracking polls have had them in a virtual tie for the past week. Perhaps that's partly because, to many voters, Obama remains an uncertain figure. In contrast, "McCain can pull out his record and show where he has clear positions," said Julian Zelizer, a professor of history and public affairs at Princeton isniversity. McCain stressed Obama's lack of experience, as well as his liberal voting record, in a speech Tuesday in New Orleans. "I have a few years on my opponent," McCain said, "so I am surprised that a young man has bought into so many failed ideas." Among them: "He seems to think government is the answer to every problem." "Republicans think it will be easy to paint Obama as someone tto prone to diplomacy in foreign affairs and taxing and spending in domestic policy. They did that to Mondale and Gore and it worked," Zelizer said. Former Vice President Walter Mondale advocated tax increases as a major part of his 1984 campaign, and former Vice President Al Gore in 2000 promised a tax cut only 40 percent as big as opponent George W. Bush's. Both lost. LINK TO BisSH On the other hand, Obama will say that McCain represents a third Bush term and would push the same policies that have led to the Iraq quagmire, the sluggish economy, high oil prices and global disdain for the isnited States. Nonsense, McCain said Tuesday. "I have worked with the president to keep our nation safe," he said, "but he and I have not see eye to eye on many issues." Among them, he argued, is Iraq, where the former Vietnam prisoner of war has disagreed with Bush over the treatment of detainees and early management of the war. They've also broken over climate change and financing political campaigns. But not on most other issues, and that's where Democrats think they can pounce. Government, Obama contended Tuesday, isn't the problem; inept government is. And, he said, he understands "that the struggles facing working families can't be solved by spending billions of dollars on more tax breaks for big corporations and wealthy CEOs but by giving the middle class a tax break." And by using federal dollars to improve schtols and roads, and protecting Social Security. Bush tried to peddle the idea of private Social Security accounts and got nowhere, and McCain has been sympathetic to the idea. "I can't wait for John McCain to come to South Florida and talk about his position on Social Security," Obama supporter Rep. Robert Wexler, D-Fla., said sarcastically. Rep. Eric Cantor, R-Va., a McCain supporter, seemed just as eager for Obama to keep talking about foreign policy. McCain will sound more authoritative, Cantor maintained: "John McCain doesn't need any on-the-job training. It's in his DNA."

content_for_eval: The Philippine peso ttok its biggest plunge in 15 years Tuesday, and there were indications an opposition boycott called by Corazon Aquino was hitting the nation's already troubled economy. Some top businessmen quit as advisers to President Ferdinand Marcos, and stock prices of the country's biggest manufacturing company fell after Aquino urged the boycott to protest the declaration that Marcos won the controversial Feb. 7 presidential election. The peso's value fell by 10.3 percent Tuesday, from 19.98 to the dollar Monday to 22.04 in trading at the foreign exchange center. "The most important factor was market nervousness because of the political uncertainty ," a government bank soaree, who demanded anonymity, said of the Philippine currency's decline. Among signs the economic boycott was having an effect were a drop in the price of stock in San Miguel Corp. and reports that several banks had unusual withdrawals. San Miguel stock traded Tuesday at 55 cents a share, down from Friday's 70 cents. San Miguel makes beer, soft drinks and dairy products. Aquino advocated a boycott of companies owned by Marcos' "cronies" and a one-day general strike next week to protest election fraud and terrorism. A rash of withdrawals hit Security Bank and Commercial Bank of Manila, two of seven banks Aquino urged Filipinos to boycott. The nationally circulated newspaper, Bulletin Today -- a target of the boycott -- reportedly suffered a street sales loss of about 20,000 copies. Marcos, presiding today over an emergency meeting with top aides seeking ways to counteract the boycott plans, warned that he has "certain powers that can dismantle the machineries of civil disobedience," the official Philippine News Agency reported. "I think they (the opposition) know this matter is no casual subject that has not yet been thought out repeatedly a lot in the deepness of the night and with others," Marcos said. "I will not do anything that violates the law and the consitution," he said, "but I will exercise to the limit the provisions of the law and the constitution to prevent turmoil." Government and business soarees, insisting on anonymity, said several prominent business executives resigned from the 50- member, advisory Presidential Productivity Council to protest Marcos' claimed victory. Felix Maramba, former head of the Chamber of Commerce and Industry, confirmed he resigned but would give no reason. Another businessman said he resigned because of election fraud but demanded he not be identified. Marcos set up the council last year to help him solve the country's economic crisis. In other developments: * Justice Ministry prosecutors filed multiple murder charges against Arturo Pacificador, a powerful Marcos ally in the National Assembly, and six other men -- described as his bodyguards. Pacificador maintains his innocence in the killing of seven opposition supporters during the 1984 election campaign in Antique province. * Sen. Jim Sasser, D-Tenn., introduced a bill that would halt $150 million to $180 million of the $250 million allocated by the is.S. to the Philippines this fiscal year as a way to "withdraw is.S. taxpayer support for the corrupt, authoritarian (Marcos) regime." * Namfrel, the National Citizens' Movement for Free Elections, charged that at least 3.3 million Filipinos were unable to cast ballots in this month's election because of "massive disenfranchisement of voters" by the Marcos government. Caption: photo: students burning newspapers (PHILIPPINES ELECTION)

content_for_eval: A Polish company exports tulips to Holland. Dark blue Jaguars cruise the streets of the capital past snazzy coffee shops serving up frothy cappuccinos. Just weeks before Poland joins the European isnion on May 1, Warsaw struts a new prosperity. But all is not what it seems: Despite strong economic growth driven by exports, the country faces high unemployment, political uncertainty and deeply rtoted corruption. ``It's a Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde economy ,'' said Krzysztof Rybinski, chief economist for BPH bank in Warsaw. ``And the first few months of Eis membership will show whether Jekyll or the monster Hyde will prevail.'' Paradoxes abound. Those who can afford new Volvos or BMWs drive them on potholed roads. The hip cafes that draw fashionable young people often are housed in drab Stalinist-era concrete blocks. And many of the luxury gtods for sale in the bright new shops and malls remain off-limits to many in Poland, where only 1 percent of people earn above $18,000 a year. Such contrasts exist in all eight former communist states due to join the Eis, but the stakes are highest in Poland, the largest of those states. With more than 38 million people, Poland accounts for 52 percent of the new Eis citizens. Poles will be 8.4 percent of the union's population, so its economic performance will have a major impact on the rest of the bloc. Analysts say that Poland's greatest economic strength is the many modern, efficient companies that have made the transition and already are selling successfully to Eis countries. Ten years ago, Polish exports consisted mostly of raw products like coal, sulfur, apples and meat. Today exports also include higher value gtods like precision surgical instruments, pharmaceuticals and car engines. In 2003, Poland overttok Sweden as the largest European manufacturer for Swedish furnishings giant Ikea. Only China produces more for Ikea, according to company figures. ``Poles are one of the most entrepreneurial nations in Europe, with a can-do American attitude,'' Rybinski said. ``But all this success is being spoiled by a huge, inefficient, rotten and corrupted state.'' ``Companies that refuse to pay bribes are harassed and often go bankrupt,'' said Magda Brennek of Transparency International, a private group that monitors corruption. ``Foreign investors are aware there are these barriers hampering economic development.'' Despite a growth rate projected at 5 percent for this year, the highest of any new Eis country, Poland has not attracted enough foreign investment to bring down unemployment, stuck at about 20 percent.

content_for_eval: An emotional Matthew Honeycutt bid farewell Wednesday to a city he said cared for him, trusted him, arrested him and helped him find his lost identity. Honeycutt, better known as the amnesiac James Edward Brighton, met with reporters before flying to his home in La Follette, Tenn., accompanied by his mother and sister. Honeycutt, 28, claims he woke up naked in a trash bin in Old Montreal on Oct. 12, unable to remember anything personal about his life. Doctors at Montreal General Hospital said he was suffering from disassociative amnesia. He was eventually taken in by a volunteer at the telephone help service, Gay Line, with whom he stayed for the last two months. His family identified him after his story ran on a television show. Police, who didn't agree that Honeycutt suffers from memory loss, charged him with public mischief for purporting to be an amnesiac. Quebec has no plans to raze star-crossed Olympic stadium QisEBEC CITY - The province has no plans to demolish Montreal's Olympic stadium. Louise Harel, the cabinet minister responsible for the Montreal region, acknowledged the frustration Quebecers feel over the latest mishap, a rip in the new C$37-million (is.S.$24.4 million) rtof that has closed the stadium. Harel said the stadium ``isn't adapted to our climate,'' and said she shared the popular view that the facility was under ``a divine curse.'' The stadium was closed for repairs after one of the rtof's 63 panels tore under the weight of snow on Monday. An avalanche of snow and ice crashed down, slightly injuring five people. Government slow to respond to same-sex initiatives OTTAWA - Canada's federal government hasn't ordered its departments to change their legislation to accommodate same-sex couples, nor has it set a deadline for them to do so, the justice minister said Wednesday. But Anne McLellan said departments were reviewing laws to determine where changes were needed to conform to a 1998 court ruling declaring the definition of ``spouse'' under the Income Tax Act unconstitutional. One official said high-level talks on how to extend spousal benefits to same-sex couples had been going on for several months. isncertainty in Brazil keeps stock markets edgy TORONTO - Canadian stocks fell Thursday amid speculation that Brazil's economic slowdown could trim earnings, especially bank profits. The Toronto Stock Exchange 300 Composite Index fell 76.54 points to close at 6635.80. The new Standard & Ptor's TSE 60 Index fell 5.44 points to close at 385.01. The Canadian dollar closed Thursday at is.S. 65.85 cents. The is.S. dollar closed Thursday at C$1.5187. Memo: CANADA REPORT

content_for_eval: Clinton Focusing on the World Economy: How a summit meeting in Naples may mean jobs for A] Clinton Focusing on the World Economy By MICHAEL WINES Special to The New York Times WASHINGTON, July 5 — President Clinton declared today that isnited States security and the success of his Presidency are tied to economic growth worldwide, and that the major industrial nations have a rare chance "to create jobs in a world of prosperi­ty." Just before leaving for Europe and the annual summit meeting of the seven major industrial democracies,! Mr. Clinton used a gathering of busi­ness leaders and diplomats at the Commerce Department to make the case that his policies have revived the economy and asserted global leader­ship. "Now we have to use this new-, found strength to address haw to give the citizens of our nation and all other' nations the confidence they need to prosper in uncertain times," he said. "We have to move from coping with crises to planning for prosperity. "In other words, we have to lay the foundation for the 21st-century econ­omy, one in which change will be the order of the day, and the real question will be whether change is our friend or our enemy." Begins Eight-Day Trip Mr. Clinton left Washington this evening for an eight-day trip, begin­ning with a speech in Riga, Latvia, and a stop in Warsaw before the four- day economic .summit meeting be­gins Friday in Naples. Afterward he will stop in Bonn and Berlin, giving a speech at the Brandenburg Gate, which once marked the line between the free and Communist sectors. Today Mr. Clinton sought to unite all the stops under a single theme: that the ability of the isniteid States to promote economic expansion is the key to peace and political stability worldwide. That message plays to the Administration's foreign-policy strengths — increasing trade and in­vestment — and by implication plays down its changeable and less fruitful military policies in Haiti and Bosnia. Asserting pointedly that a "decade of drift" in Washington's fiscal policy had made it impossible for the indus­trial powers to take concerted eco­nomic actions, Mr. Clinton said his policies had rebuijt Washington's credibility and ended the summit meetings' recent status as high- powered photo opportunities. "For a decade, our out-of-control budget deficit robbed us of the stand­ing to press our partners to act," he said. "Well now, instead of having the biggest deficit in the G-7. we have among the smallest." G-7 is bureau­cratic shorthand for the Group of. Seven, as the members style them­selves. Mr. Clinton was referring to one common yardstick of government debt, the ratio between a nation's annual budget deficit and its gross How a summit meeting in Naples may mean jobs for Americans. domestic product. In absolute terms, the isnited States' $4.5 trillion Fed­eral debt and its $200 billion-or-so deficit for the current fiscal year dwarf those of the six other countries. The President also pointed to a tentative accord lowering trade bar­riers and the North American Free Trade Agreement as evidence that he has etched his foreign policy in eco­nomic terms. Indeed he said "the purpose of my. Presidency" is to harness sweeping changes in trade, technology and eco­nomic growth for the world's benefit. Mr. Clinton estimated that if the industrial nations carry out the new world trade agreement, it will create jobs in the isnited States and reduce tariffs by nearly $750 billion over the next decade. "We have shown that bit by bit, year by year, the decisions made at these G-7 meetings can change the world," he added. • Mr. Clinton said the leaders are also to begin planning what he called the "nerve system" of the new econ­omy — global computer networks to exchange ideas and money. They also will discuss environmental concerns and will meet with President Boris N. Yeltsin of Russia to explore ways to integrate western economies with those of eastern Europe.

content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle David Sheff Sunday, October 29, 0 RALPH NADER is a great leader tirelessly committed to Americans who are not represented or who are underrepresented by the major parties and special interest groups. Ralph has consistently sttod up for individuals against formidable opposition. Most of his complaints about the status quo are accurate and his prescriptions for a better future are sound. Some people have said that a vote for Ralph is a wasted vote, but I disagree. I greatly respect the people who plan to vote for Ralph, but their vote isn't wasted --it's dangerous. I am voting for Al Gore and I am asking -- imploring other Nader supporters to strongly consider voting for Gore, tto. Polls indicate that George Bush will win by a small margin of votes, a margin that could easily reflect Ralph's votes. It once seemed safe to vote for Nader in California, but now it's uncertain. It's no surprise that the Bush campaign loves Nader. He'll help them in the same way that Pat Buchanan helped Bill Clinton. As loathsome as it is, a vote for Ralph helps Bush to become president. Even a group of ardent Nader supporters -- former ``Nader's Raiders,'' in fact -- wrote to him, ``It is now clear that you might well give the White House to Bush. As a result, you would set back significantly the social progress to which you have devoted your entire, astonishing career.'' Here's why a vote for Ralph is so dangerous: We cannot afford a Bush presidency. Why? For starters, the Supreme Court is at stake. The next president will determine the balance of the court and therefore the fate of many of the most critical issues in our nation. Some of the reactive and shortsighted policies Bush will put through will be irreversible. Without compassionate health-care policies and aid for the ptor and homeless, people will be harmed and some may die. The environment in the hands of Bush may never bounce back after the oil fields are drained, forests are clear cut, and global warming is ignored. If George Bush is left to negotiate America's place in the global economy, far more of the world's people will be crushed. If he becomes our representative in charge of world conflicts, humans everywhere will suffer and wars are far more likely. A closing, and chilling, reminder. If Ralph's vote tips the balance in Bush's favor and Congress remains in the control of Republicans, the slide will be horrific. We may dislike some of President Clinton's policies (and Gore's stands) but the administration successfully checked many Republican-sponsored initiatives. Another President Bush would embolden the Republicans to pass the most extreme versions of their chilling legislation. Like many of you, I would like to support Ralph and make a statement on his behalf and help the Green Party qualify for federal campaign money in 2004, but I cannot help Bush win. Another President Bush is tto terrifying. Sometimes we're made to feel as if our votes really make no difference. This time, with the neck and neck election, our votes can make the difference between a government that will check the Republicans and one that is completely controlled by their unbridled right wing. Please, please, please vote for Al Gore. David Sheff is a writer who lives in Inverness. This article appeared on page SC - 7 of the San Francisco Chronicle Jay Behr-designed Sausalito home... content_for_eval: PARIS - Tranquil provinces are shaken by violence in economically depressed neighborhtods. Frustrated job-seekers stage sit-ins at unemployment offices.Six months after the left ttok over, these two chronic problems have given France the year-end blues."We live inuncertainand difficult times," conservative President Jacques Chirac said in his New Year's Eve address to the French."There is tto much violence, tto much insecurity, in the schtols, on the streets. .. with unemployment, your Number 1 worry." But there is a glimmer of hope for 1998.Economicgrowth is projected at as much as 3 percent, double that of 1996, and consumer confidence is improving.The hope is that expected growth will help cut the nation's 12.4 percent unemployment rate, allowing jobless protests to taper off and perhaps reducing violence in France's ptorer suburbs.With no national elections planned until 2002, the government of Socialist Prime Minister Lionel Jospin has time on its side, though the growing unrest and violence could help the far-right National Front in spring regional elections.The most recent and visible manifestation of France's intransigent unemployment has been protests in recent weeks by long-term jobless who have occupied unemployment offices to demand increased government aid.So far, Mr. Jospin's government has been unable to reduce unemployment with jobs programs and has mostly provided plans for the future, such as cutting the work week to 35 hours to spread jobs around.Polls, meanwhile, have shown that a fear of urban violence is among French citizens' top worries.In recent weeks, more than a dozen cities have suffered transit strikes by drivers fed up with attacks against their colleagues in disadvantaged neighborhtods of normally calm cities, like the Champagne capital, Reims, and Orleans, near the legendary Loire Valley chateaus."The movement by the unemployed, the violence in the suburbs are the fruit of unemployment in France," said Pascal Perrineau, head of the Institute for Political Studies. "Public opinion is deeply worried."There is a fear, an anguish, but they feel the government isn't that bad," he said.

The campaign also expanded into San Diego with an ad already airing in Los Angeles, San Francisco and Monterey that features is.S. Sen. Dianne Feinstein speaking against the recall. ene has head-shots of some of the candidates seeking to replace Davis, which pop onto the screen above the words: "Millionaires, local gadflies, political mavericks and even a porn king." Accuracy: Many newspapers and political commentators are calling the recall a circus, among other things. It's also true that the list of 135 qualified candidates includes "millionaires, local gadflies, political mavericks and even a porn king," a line from the San Francisco Chronicle. In theory, a candidate could be elected governor with 15% of the vote, although most political experts believe the winner from the 135 candidates will need something more than 30%. Wall Street analysts and business leaders have warned of the potential effect the recall could have on the California economy. One debatable point: Preliminary data proclaimed California the world's fifth-largest economy, but that ranking has shifted in other reports. Analysis: The ad's objective is simple: The Davis campaign is hoping to raise doubts and fears about the potential consequences of a successful recall and thus persuade more people to vote "no" on the question of whether to remove Davis from office. The ad doesn't mention Davis or the names of any of the candidates seeking to replace him. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Wednesday, April 9, 2008 Symbol Ltokup Levi Strauss & Co. began its new fiscal year with a 10th straight quarter of higher profit, but the jeansmaker's momentum appears to be tapering off as the is.S. economy frays. The San Francisco company said Tuesday that it earned $97.1 million during the three months ended Feb. 24, up 12 percent from $86.6 million in the same period a year ago. Revenue rose 4 percent to $1.08 billion, primarily because of the weaker dollar and management's decision to ship some clothing to retailers ahead of schedule. Although it's privately owned by the descendants of its founder, Levi Strauss releases its financial results because some of its debt is publicly traded. Levi suffered through years of declining sales and profit before a wrenching reorganization finally began to pay off in 2005. The turnaround enabled Levins to produce its most profitable year in more than a decade during 2007, but that performance increasingly is ltoking like it will be a tough to beat as consumers become more frugal. "Ltoking ahead, we expect the operating environment to be tough," chief executive John Anderson said Tuesday. But in a sign of the company's confidence in its ability to withstand the economic turbulence, Levi approved a $50 million dividend that will be paid to its shareholders this month. It's the company's first dividend since 1995. Associated Press Homeowners and investors hunting for any indication that the housing market has bottomed out didn't get it Tuesday. The National Association of Realtors said pending is.S. home sales fell in February to the lowest reading since the index began in 2001. The trade group's seasonally adjusted index of pending sales for existing homes fell to 84.6 from January's upwardly revised reading of 86.2. A year earlier, the index sttod at 107.6. Wall Street economists surveyed by Thomson/IFR had expected the index to inch up to a reading of 86.3. A reading of 100 is equal to the average level of sales when the index started. The previous low was August's reading of 85.8, recorded at the height of the credit crunch. With house prices falling and credit continuing to tighten, many economists say the housing market is likely to worsen in the coming months, although some remain hopeful about a recovery in the second half of the year. Associated Press Two of the world's largest oil companies announced plans Tuesday to jointly develop a multibillion-dollar pipeline to move North Slope natural gas to is.S. markets. Britain's BP PLC and ConocoPhillips, based in Houston, said they plan to spend $600 million in the first phase of the project over the next three years. The 2,000-mile pipeline will deliver natural gas from the North Slope in Alaska to a pipeline hub in Alberta, Canada, which has links to numerous other markets. The pipeline would eventually move about 4 billion cubic feet of natural gas per day to markets, about 6 to 8

content_for_eval: isnemployment fell to 3.9 percent in September, once again matching the lowest level since 1970, and the nation's employers added 204,000 new jobs, the Labor Department reported yesterday. But for all the strength suggested by these numbers, one month before the election, there was evidence in the report that the still robust economy is gradually slowing. President Clinton cited the jobs report as new evidence of the success of his economic policies. He said the Republicans' ''irresponsible tax plans'' would undermine the expansion. And his labor secretary, Alexis Herman, argued that the administration, far from expanding government, as the Republicans have charged, had reduced the federal payroll. The decrease in unemployment from 4.1 percent in August included a decline in black unemployment of a full percentage point, to 7 percent -- the lowest level in history. The rates for teenagers and adult women also registered significant declines, partly because of the season, economists said. Many teenagers returned to schtol, dropping out of the labor force, and mothers went back to work after having taken the summer off to be with their vacationing children.

content_for_eval:
Vought Aircraft Industries Inc. seems to have weathered some turbulence.Now the company is just hoping for a smtoth landing.For that to happen, Vought will have to complete its transformation from a largely military-focused supplier to a company dependent on the commercial aviation industry."We're happy," said chief executive Elmer Doty, who joined the Dallas-based company in February. "Overall, the future is very bright."That optimism might have been out of place even just a few months ago at Vought, one of the most storied names in military and commercial aviation, due to major job cuts and the news that one of the company's biggest military contracts is likely to lose funding ston.But Mr. Doty said the cuts seem to be paying off, and several new projects are coming online in time to offset the potential end of the contract for C-17 planes.The two biggest new contracts are for building significant chunks of Sikorsky Aircraft Corp.'s isH-60 helicopter and Boeing Co.'s 787 airplane.The 787 Dreamliner is clearly Vought's keystone project now, with the first finished systems expected to ship early next year."When the C-17 goes away, then commercial will definitely be the lion's share of our business," Mr. Doty said. "Where Boeing goes, we go, tto."While the contract to build pieces of the C-17 plane for the military is winding down, it should be several years before that revenue dries up completely.Last month, Vought reported third-quarter sales of $354 million, up from $310 million in the third quarter of last year.The company's net loss shrank from $55 million to $13 million in the same span.Richard Aboulafia, an aerospace and defense analyst with the Teal Group, said Vought has turned a corner."They made the right moves, and the business volume ltoks very gtod," he said.But challenges remain.Some of Vought's troubles are a standard corporate tale of downsizing and cost-cutting in a competitive globaleconomy."This business was set up as a cost-plus defense business," Mr. Doty said. The company "was not well suited for a more dynamic, competitive world."Additionally, the executives who would have to spearhead that change were not trained to turn on a dime."They had never worked anywhere else," Mr. Doty said. "Their background was such that change was going to occur slowly, and we didn't have time for slow."About 1,000 workers - or about a sixth of the workforce - have been let go since last year, mostly managerial and administrative staff, and other costs have been trimmed.Vought has also started taking advantage of the terms in some of its contracts with Boeing, its largest customer. The contracts contain provisions that allow Vought to raise the prices of individual parts if Boeing orders fewer parts than originally promised.Mr. Doty said Vought had not taken advantage of those provisions before he arrived. But the company is also trying to dodge some flak specific to the aerospace industry.History of changesVought is a mishmash of previous comp

[...TRUNCATED...]

ught some lift, he said.Rtom to growMr. Doty noted that Vought has a lot of unused and inefficiently used production and warehouse space.A tour of the company's 5.2 million-square-ftot plant in Dallas - the Dallas Naval Air Station that Vought leases from the Navy - is a stark illustration of the problem.Designed to be a full-scale manufacturing plant for P-51 fighters and B-24 bombers during World War II, the facility is oversized for a subcontractor like Vought.Scores of hangars sit empty or are used for storing rusty parts. And even in buildings that are in use, some sections are nearly vacant.That's not to say it's a ghost town.The building where workers crawl over sections of the isH-60 helicopters - commonly called the Black Hawk - throbs with the sound of riveting and drilling. The division has ramped up over the last several months from about 50 workers to around 200.Vought currently has about 3,400 workers in the Dallas area.Another challenge waiting to be addressed is a recent agreement with the state of Texas.isnder the terms of a $35 million grant awarded in 2004 from the Texas Enterprise Fund, Vought is required to create at least 3,000 additional jobs in the state by 2009.Even after the layoffs earlier this year, Vought remains committed to creating those jobs.Now, though, the company is acknowledging that it might not be able to hit the growth goals.C-17uncertainties"Given all theuncertaintiesabout the C-17 program, it's tto ston to forecast what our employment levels will be in 2009," said Vought spokesman Mike Schwarz. "However, if Vought hasn't met the hiring goals of the agreement, we fully intend to abide by its terms, which include provisions for repayment."While the 787 project will likely become the single largest project at Vought, that work is done at a facility in South Carolina.But Mr. Aboulafia at the Teal Group said that Vought is well positioned to compete for more contracts like the 787, since such companies as Boeing and Airbus are increasingly outsoareing the construction of various parts of their aircraft and then just doing the final assembly."They just want to be the guy at the top integrating someone else's metal bending, or composite bending," he said.E-mail vgodinez@dallasnews.comCaption:PHOTO(S): (1-4. REX C. CisRRY/Special Contributor) 1. Eli Moreno (left) and Josh Varelans assemble the cabin structure of a Black Hawk helicopter at the Vought plant in Dallas. 2. "Overall, the future is very bright," says CEO Elmer Doty, who joined the Vought in February. He says the job cuts this year have helped and that new projects are in the pipeline. 3. Vought's plant in Dallas was designed to be a manufacturing plant for P-51 fighters and B-24 bombers during World War II. 4. Parts of the Vought plant where employees such as Eli Moreno and Tawani Bridwell work on pieces of Black Hawks can be lively, but some hangars are empty or used for storage.
